title: Cluster&Merge
shortDesc: A collaborative brainstorming prototype that combines NLP keyword extraction, clustering, merging, and image generation to stimulate ideation.

expertise.reason: Implemented speech-to-text keyword processing and interaction logging, and analyzed interaction patterns across timed intervals after merge actions.
Built and tested a working touch-enabled prototype in Processing with keyword streams, drag/place interaction, merge behavior, image querying, and deletion logic.
Designed ideation-support interactions that combine textual and visual stimuli, including clustering and merged-image prompts to spark unconventional ideas.
Conducted a field study with 15 participants in 4 groups, including observation and interviews, and evaluated collaborative dynamics, inclusion, and perceived usefulness.
Project focused primarily on research and prototype evaluation rather than commercialization.

content.overview: Cluster&Merge is a collocated collaborative brainstorming system that uses natural language processing to convert speech into live keyword streams, then allows participants to cluster and merge words into new prompts. By combining touch interaction and visual image generation, the prototype explores whether merged concepts can improve ideation quality and collaboration during brainstorm sessions.
keyFeatures: Word clustering and merge-triggered concept generation
Image querying for single and merged keywords

sections.title: Research Focus
sections.text: The project investigated how combining speech-to-text extracted keywords and visual merging influences brainstorming quality. The core research question centered on whether merged keyword prompts and generated imagery can stimulate richer or more varied idea generation in face-to-face collaboration.

sections.title: Prototype
sections.text: The system was built in Processing and run on a touch-enabled LCD setup. Spoken input is translated into keyword streams that participants can drag, place, cluster, and merge. A merge action combines words into a new concept and allows image retrieval for additional inspiration. A trash boundary enables quick removal of irrelevant items.

media.caption: prototype device (tv screen) that consist an LCD screen with a touch input layer on top
How the actual screen looked, dragging a word out of the stream on the prototype. Dragging multiple words over eachother would merge them and query images

sections.title: User Study
sections.text: A field study was conducted with 15 graduate design participants in 4 groups. Sessions included an introduction, a timed brainstorm, and individual debrief interviews. Findings showed mixed but generally positive responses: participants appreciated playful collaboration and inspiration from images, while others reported distraction, pacing issues, and occasional disruption of conversational flow.

media.caption: Picture of camera taped to the ceiling during field study.
The second computer is attached to the prototype to run the program.

sections.title: Findings & Reflection
sections.text: Clustering and merging were often used out of curiosity and perceived as engaging, but their measurable effect on interactions was not consistently positive. Merged images were considered inspiring by many participants, while others felt they could constrain imagination. The study highlighted trade-offs between stimulation and distraction, and pointed to future improvements in pacing, organization tools, and interface adaptability for different collaboration styles.
